schools.summary: The one language: 'and the whole earth was of one language, and of one speech' — before the dispersion at Babel, all mankind speaking one language (the whole human race, descended from Noah's family, having a common tongue and common words, the original unity of speech, before the confusion, v.7, the one language of the post-flood world); 'one language (one lip) and one speech (the same words)' (mankind united in a single tongue, the unity of language a bond of the unity of the race, the common speech binding the peoples together, a unity later broken at Babel as a judgment on their pride, what this original language was, some say Hebrew, variously held, but the point the unity of speech); the unity of language set at the head of the Babel narrative (the common tongue making possible the common enterprise of the tower, vv.3-4, the one speech, which should have served the glory of God, turned to a proud and rebellious project, the unity of mankind, abused, to be broken by the confusion of tongues) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And the whole earth was of one language, and of one speech
the unity of mankind in one language before Babel
all mankind united in a single tongue, the bond of the race
the common speech, the setting for the tower

schools.summary: The journey to Shinar: 'and it came to pass, as they journeyed from the east, that they found a plain in the land of Shinar; and they dwelt there' — mankind, migrating (from the east, or eastward, the Hebrew variously rendered), coming to the plain of Shinar (Babylonia, lower Mesopotamia, the movement of the post-flood race, the settling in the fertile plain of Shinar); they finding 'a plain (a wide valley) in the land of Shinar' (the broad, fertile plain of Babylonia, well-watered and suited for settlement, the plain of Shinar, where Nimrod's kingdom was, 10:10, and where the tower would be built, the place chosen for the settlement and the proud enterprise); 'they dwelt there' (mankind settling in the plain of Shinar, instead of spreading out to fill the earth, as God purposed, 9:1, they gathering and dwelling together in one place, the settling in Shinar setting the stage for the building of the city and tower, vv.3-4, the project that sought to keep them united against the dispersion) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, as they journeyed from the east, that they found a plain in the land of Shinar; and they dwelt there
the journey to the plain of Shinar
the post-flood race settling in the fertile plain of Babylonia
gathering in one place rather than spreading to fill the earth

schools.summary: The making of brick: 'and they said one to another, Go to, let us make brick, and burn them throughly. And they had brick for stone, and slime had they for morter' — the builders preparing materials for their project (lacking stone in the alluvial plain of Shinar, they making bricks and burning, firing, them thoroughly for hardness and durability, the deliberate, organized preparation for the building of the city and tower); 'they had brick for stone, and slime (bitumen) had they for morter' (in the plain of Babylonia, which lacks stone, they using fired brick instead of stone, and bitumen, slime, asphalt, plentiful in that region, instead of mortar, the materials of Babylonian building, confirmed by the brick-and-bitumen construction of ancient Mesopotamia, the durable materials for the great structure); the careful preparation of brick and bitumen showing the deliberate, ambitious nature of the project (mankind uniting their skill and labor in a great building enterprise, the organized human effort, soon to be turned to a proud and rebellious end, v.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And they said one to another, Go to, let us make brick, and burn them throughly. And they had brick for stone, and slime had they for morter
the making of brick for the building
the brick-and-bitumen construction of Babylonia
the deliberate, organized human enterprise

schools.summary: Calvin and Gill on the sin of pride and self-exaltation: 'let us make us a name' — the builders seeking fame, a name, a monument to their own glory (the pride of man, exalting himself and seeking renown apart from God); the city and tower a great monument of human pride and power; 'whose top may reach unto heaven' a hyperbole for an exceedingly high tower ('for there is nothing for men impossible,' not a literal reaching to heaven), some holding the tower a temple (a ziggurat), perhaps for idolatrous worship (some Jewish writers); the sin of Babel chiefly the pride and ambition of man, exalting himself and his name against God.

The grammatical-historical and Henry on defiance of God's command: 'lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth' — the builders seeking to keep themselves united in one place, against God's command to fill the earth (9:1), the refusal to be scattered, the resistance to God's purpose for the dispersion of mankind, the heart of their rebellion; their express purpose to avoid being scattered, to remain united, build a centre, and make a name, direct resistance to God's will that mankind spread and fill the earth, their God-defying union and pride calling down the judgment (vv.5-9); some (Jewish writers) holding the tower for idolatry or for security (against another flood, or as a centre of empire under Nimrod, 10:10), the various aspects — pride, ambition, defiance of God's command to disperse, and possibly idolatry — making up the sin of Babel — Ellicott, Henry, Gill, Clarke.

debates.question: What was the sin of Babel, and does the tower literally reach heaven?

sides.position: Pride and self-exaltation — seeking to 'make us a name,' a monument to human glory apart from God; 'top reaching heaven' a hyperbole for a very high tower
Defiance of God's command to fill the earth — the refusal to be scattered, resisting the dispersion of mankind (9:1)
Idolatry/false worship or self-security — the tower a temple (ziggurat), or a refuge against another flood / a centre of empire

themes: And they said, Go to, let us build us a city and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven; and let us make us a name, lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth
the building of the city and tower (the crux of the sin of Babel)
pride and a name vs defiance of the command to fill the earth vs idolatry
the 'top reaching heaven' a hyperbole for a very high tower

schools.summary: The LORD comes down to see: 'and the LORD came down to see the city and the tower, which the children of men builded' — God represented (after the manner of men) as 'coming down' to inspect the work of the builders (not that God, who is everywhere and sees all, must come down or go to see, an anthropomorphism, but the language marking God's taking notice of, and judicial investigation of, the proud enterprise, God observing the work of men); the irony — the builders sought to reach heaven with their tower, yet God must 'come down' to see their puny work (the contrast between their proud ambition and the smallness of their structure in God's sight, God's 'coming down to see' marking his judicial inquiry before he acts, as before Sodom, 18:21, the divine inspection that precedes the judgment); 'the children of men' (the sons of men, perhaps marking them as merely men, or as the godless, in contrast to the sons of God, the proud work of mortal men, which God beholds, the structure of 'the children of men,' soon to be confounded by God's judgment) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD came down to see the city and the tower, which the children of men builded
the LORD's coming down to see the city and tower (an anthropomorphism)
God's judicial notice of the proud enterprise before judgment
the irony that the heaven-reaching tower required God to come down to see it

schools.summary: God's word on the united people: 'and the LORD said, Behold, the people is one, and they have all one language; and this they begin to do: and now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do' — God observing the unity of mankind: one people, one language (their unity of race and speech giving them great power for their common enterprise, the unity that should have served God being used for a proud, rebellious project); 'this they begin to do: and now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do' (their united strength and one mind meaning that, having begun this, nothing they imagined would be beyond them, their unbridled ambition, if unchecked, leading to ever greater pride and evil, God noting the danger of their united, unrestrained will, the bold imagination of man, capable of great evil when united); God's word marking the need to restrain man's united pride (the confusion of tongues, v.7, checking their unrestrained ambition by breaking their unity, God, in mercy and judgment, scattering them, lest their united evil grow unchecked, the restraining of man's imagined evil by the dispersion) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said, Behold, the people is one, and they have all one language... now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do
God's word on the united people and their unrestrained imagination
the united, unbridled ambition of man, capable of great evil
the need to restrain man's pride by the confusion of tongues and dispersion

schools.summary: Gill and the dominant Christian reading (the Trinity): 'Go to, let us go down' taken as the language of the Three Persons of the Godhead — God speaking, as it were, within himself, the plural marking the Trinity (as in 1:26, 'Let us make man'), the Three Persons subsisting in one Essence, deliberating and acting together in the judgment, the dominant reading seeing here, with 1:26, a proof of the plurality of Persons in the one God; a second reading (the Jewish, the Targum, Aben Ezra) taking 'let us' as God speaking to the angels (addressing the heavenly council), but it being objected that the angels could not share in this divine work of confounding tongues (a work of God alone); a third reading taking 'let us' as the plural of majesty or deliberation (God deliberating with himself); God resolving to 'confound their language' (to confuse their speech, so that they could not understand one another, the sudden diversity of tongues breaking their communication, halting their work, and forcing their dispersion, the confusion of tongues the means of God's judgment on their pride, the origin of the many languages of mankind) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: Whom does God address in 'let us go down'?

sides.position: The Three Persons of the Godhead — the plural marking the Trinity (with 1:26, the dominant Christian reading)
The angels / the heavenly council (the Jewish/Targum reading) — objected to, since confounding tongues is God's work alone
The plural of majesty or deliberation (God deliberating with himself)

themes: Go to, let us go down, and there confound their language, that they may not understand one another's speech
God's resolve to confound their language (the crux of 'let us go down')
the Three Persons of the Godhead vs the angels vs the plural of majesty
the confusion of tongues, the judgment on the pride of Babel

schools.summary: The scattering: 'so the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth: and they left off to build the city' — by the confusion of tongues, God scattering mankind from Babel over the whole earth (the very dispersion they sought to avoid, v.4, now brought upon them by God's judgment, the peoples, no longer able to understand one another, separating and spreading over the earth, the origin of the dispersion of the nations); the irony — the builders sought to make a name and avoid being scattered (v.4), yet God scattered them and brought their pride to nothing (the very thing they feared coming upon them by their own sin, God's purpose to fill the earth, 9:1, accomplished, against their will, by the judgment, the providence of God overruling man's pride); 'they left off to build the city' (the confusion of tongues halting the work, unable to understand one another, the builders abandoning the city and tower unfinished, the proud enterprise broken off, the monument of human pride left incomplete, the judgment effectively stopping the rebellion) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: So the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth: and they left off to build the city
the scattering of mankind and the halting of the city
the dispersion they feared brought upon them by God's judgment
the proud work halted and left unfinished

schools.summary: The naming of Babel: 'therefore is the name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth: and from thence did the LORD scatter them abroad upon the face of all the earth' — the city called Babel ('confusion'), the name (from the Hebrew balal, 'to confound') commemorating the confusion of tongues there ('Babel,' which the builders perhaps intended as 'gate of God,' Bab-el, becoming, by God's judgment, 'confusion,' the name recording the divine judgment); the name given 'because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth' (at Babel, God confounding the one language into many, the origin of the diversity of human tongues, the confusion of languages, the means and memorial of God's judgment on the pride of Babel); 'from thence did the LORD scatter them abroad upon the face of all the earth' (from Babel, God scattering mankind over the whole earth, the dispersion of the nations springing from this place and event, the peoples, divided by tongue, spreading to fill the earth, Babel the origin of the nations' dispersion, 10:32) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Therefore is the name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth
the naming of Babel and the confusion of tongues
Babel ('confusion,' balal), the memorial of God's judgment on pride
the origin of the diversity of tongues and the dispersion of the nations

schools.summary: The generations of Shem: 'these are the generations of Shem: Shem was an hundred years old, and begat Arphaxad two years after the flood' — the formula introducing the genealogy of Shem (vv.10-26), tracing the line of promise from Shem to Abram (after the digression of the Table of Nations, ch. 10, and Babel, vv.1-9, the narrative returning to the chosen line, the genealogy that leads to Abraham); Shem begetting Arphaxad (the head of the line of promise, 10:24) two years after the flood (the genealogy of the chosen line beginning with Shem and Arphaxad, the line through which the Messiah would come, dated to the years after the flood); the genealogy of Shem narrowing from the nations (ch. 10) to the line of promise (from Shem through Arphaxad, Salah, Eber, and onward to Abram, v.26, the careful tracing of the chosen line, God's faithfulness in preserving the line through which the promised seed would come) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: These are the generations of Shem: Shem was an hundred years old, and begat Arphaxad two years after the flood
the heading of the generations of Shem, the line of promise
the chosen line narrowing from the nations to the line of the Messiah
traced from Shem to Abram

schools.summary: Shem's long life: 'and Shem lived after he begat Arphaxad five hundred years, and begat sons and daughters' — Shem living five hundred years after Arphaxad's birth (a total of six hundred years, among the long-lived patriarchs of the post-flood line, though not so long as the antediluvians, Shem begetting many other children besides Arphaxad, the long life of the head of the chosen line); Shem's six hundred years (still a great age) part of the pattern of the post-flood line, in which the lifespans, though long, begin to shorten from the antediluvian ages (ch. 5, Shem living long, seeing many generations of his descendants, even, by the chronology, to the days of Abraham); 'begat sons and daughters' (Shem having many children besides Arphaxad, the line of promise traced through Arphaxad, but the many other offspring noted, the multiplying of Shem's line, the increase of the chosen family) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Shem lived after he begat Arphaxad five hundred years, and begat sons and daughters
Shem's long life and his many children
the long life of the head of the line of promise
the lifespans beginning to shorten from the antediluvian ages

schools.summary: Arphaxad begets Salah: 'and Arphaxad lived five and thirty years, and begat Salah' — Arphaxad (son of Shem, the head of the line of promise) begetting Salah at thirty-five years (the line continuing from Shem through Arphaxad to Salah, the chosen line carried forward toward Abram); Arphaxad begetting at thirty-five — notably younger than the antediluvians (who often begat their first-named son at sixty-five or more, ch. 5, the age of begetting beginning to shorten in the post-flood line, as do the total lifespans, the genealogy carefully tracing the chosen line); 'begat Salah' (Salah, the son of Arphaxad, the next in the line of promise, and the father of Eber, v.14, the chosen line carried from Arphaxad to Salah, the careful record of the generations through which the promise would come, the line of faith traced toward Abram) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Arphaxad lived five and thirty years, and begat Salah
Arphaxad begetting Salah, continuing the line of promise
the line carried from Shem through Arphaxad to Salah
the age of begetting now shorter than the antediluvians'

schools.summary: Arphaxad's long life: 'and Arphaxad lived after he begat Salah four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters' — Arphaxad living four hundred and three years after Salah's birth (a total of four hundred thirty-eight years, a long life, though shorter than Shem's, Arphaxad begetting many other children besides Salah, the continuing of the chosen line and the multiplying of the family); Arphaxad's total of four hundred thirty-eight years part of the post-flood pattern, in which the lifespans, though still long, are shorter than the antediluvians' (ch. 5) and shorter than Shem's (the gradual shortening of human life after the flood, the long but diminishing lifespans of the chosen line); 'begat sons and daughters' (Arphaxad having many children besides Salah, the line of promise traced through Salah, but the many other offspring noted, the multiplying of the chosen family, the increase of Shem's line) — Gill.

themes: And Arphaxad lived after he begat Salah four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters
Arphaxad's long life and his many children
the long but diminishing lifespans of the chosen line
the line of promise carried through Salah, the family multiplying

schools.summary: Salah begets Eber: 'and Salah lived thirty years, and begat Eber' — Salah (son of Arphaxad) begetting Eber at thirty years (the line of promise continuing from Arphaxad through Salah to Eber, the chosen line carried forward toward Abram, Eber, from whom the Hebrews are named, 10:21); Salah begetting at thirty — younger still than Arphaxad (v.12, the age of begetting continuing to shorten in the post-flood line, the genealogy carefully tracing the chosen line through the generations); 'begat Eber' (Eber, the son of Salah, the next in the line of promise, from whom the name 'Hebrew' is derived, 10:21, the ancestor of Peleg, v.16, and of Abram, the line of faith carried through Eber, the head of the Hebrew line in the genealogy of promise) — Gill.

themes: And Salah lived thirty years, and begat Eber
Salah begetting Eber, continuing the line of promise
the line carried from Arphaxad through Salah to Eber
Eber, from whom the Hebrews are named (10:21)

schools.summary: Salah's long life: 'and Salah lived after he begat Eber four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters' — Salah living four hundred and three years after Eber's birth (a total of four hundred thirty-three years, a long life in the post-flood line, Salah begetting many other children besides Eber, the continuing and multiplying of the chosen line); Salah's long life part of the post-flood pattern, the lifespans still long but gradually shortening from the antediluvian ages (ch. 5, the long but diminishing lifespans of the chosen line, Salah, like the fathers before him, living to see many generations); 'begat sons and daughters' (Salah having many children besides Eber, the line of promise traced through Eber, but the many other offspring noted, the multiplying of the chosen family, the increase of Shem's line through Salah) — Gill.

themes: And Salah lived after he begat Eber four hundred and three years, and begat sons and daughters
Salah's long life and his many children
the long but diminishing lifespans of the chosen line
the line of promise carried through Eber, the family multiplying

schools.summary: Eber begets Peleg: 'and Eber lived four and thirty years, and begat Peleg' — Eber (the head of the Hebrew line, 10:21) begetting Peleg at thirty-four years (the line of promise continuing from Salah through Eber to Peleg, Peleg, in whose days the earth was divided, 10:25, the next in the chosen line); Eber begetting Peleg at thirty-four (the line of promise carried through Peleg, not Joktan, 10:25, the genealogy tracing the chosen line through Eber's son Peleg toward Abram); 'begat Peleg' (Peleg, 'division,' in whose days the earth was divided, the dispersion of the nations at Babel, 10:25, the very event of vv.1-9, the line of promise running through Peleg, the genealogy connecting the line of Shem with the Babel dispersion, Peleg's name commemorating it, the chosen line carried through Peleg toward Abram) — Gill.

themes: And Eber lived four and thirty years, and begat Peleg
Eber begetting Peleg, in whose days the earth was divided
the line of promise carried from Salah through Eber to Peleg
connecting the chosen line with the Babel dispersion (10:25)

schools.summary: Eber's long life: 'and Eber lived after he begat Peleg four hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters' — Eber living four hundred and thirty years after Peleg's birth (a total of four hundred sixty-four years, a long life, indeed the longest of the post-flood line after Shem, some noting Eber outlived several generations of his descendants, Eber begetting many other children besides Peleg, the continuing and multiplying of the chosen line); Eber's total of four hundred sixty-four years a great age, longer than his fathers Salah and Arphaxad (the post-flood lifespans, though gradually shortening, still long, Eber, the head of the Hebrew line, living long, seeing many generations); 'begat sons and daughters' (Eber having many children besides Peleg, including Joktan, 10:25, the line of promise traced through Peleg, but the many other offspring, and the Arabian line of Joktan, noted, the multiplying of the chosen family) — Gill.

themes: And Eber lived after he begat Peleg four hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters
Eber's long life and his many children
the great age of the head of the Hebrew line
the line of promise carried through Peleg, the family multiplying

schools.summary: Peleg begets Reu: 'and Peleg lived thirty years, and begat Reu' — Peleg (in whose days the earth was divided, 10:25) begetting Reu at thirty years (the line of promise continuing from Eber through Peleg to Reu, the chosen line carried forward toward Abram); Peleg begetting Reu at thirty, and (as v.19 notes) Peleg's total lifespan being only two hundred thirty-nine years — a marked shortening from his fathers (Eber's four hundred sixty-four, the post-flood lifespans now dropping sharply, from over four hundred to around two hundred years, the gradual decline of human life after the flood); 'begat Reu' (Reu, the son of Peleg, the next in the line of promise, the chosen line carried from Peleg to Reu, the careful record of the generations through which the promise would come, the line of faith traced toward Abram, with the lifespans now markedly shorter) — Gill.

themes: And Peleg lived thirty years, and begat Reu
Peleg begetting Reu, continuing the line of promise
the line carried from Eber through Peleg to Reu
the lifespans now dropping markedly (Peleg's 239 years)

schools.summary: Peleg's shortened life: 'and Peleg lived after he begat Reu two hundred and nine years, and begat sons and daughters' — Peleg living two hundred and nine years after Reu's birth (a total of two hundred thirty-nine years, a marked shortening from his fathers, Eber's four hundred sixty-four, the post-flood lifespans now dropping to around two hundred years, Peleg begetting many other children besides Reu); Peleg's total of two hundred thirty-nine years much shorter than the generations before him (the lifespans of the chosen line, long after the flood, now declining sharply toward the shorter spans of Abraham's day, 175 years, 25:7, and after, the gradual diminishing of human life by God's appointment); 'begat sons and daughters' (Peleg having many children besides Reu, the line of promise traced through Reu, but the many other offspring noted, the multiplying of the chosen family even as the lifespans shorten) — Gill.

themes: And Peleg lived after he begat Reu two hundred and nine years, and begat sons and daughters
Peleg's life and his many children
the marked decline of human life, toward Abraham's day
the line of promise carried through Reu

schools.summary: Reu begets Serug: 'and Reu lived two and thirty years, and begat Serug' — Reu (son of Peleg) begetting Serug at thirty-two years (the line of promise continuing from Peleg through Reu to Serug, the chosen line carried forward toward Abram, the lifespans now in the shorter post-flood pattern, Reu's total, 239 years, v.21); Reu begetting Serug at thirty-two (the line of promise carried through Serug, the genealogy tracing the chosen line, with the lifespans now around two hundred years, the steady narrowing toward Abram); 'begat Serug' (Serug, the son of Reu, the next in the line of promise, and the grandfather of Terah, the father of Abram, the chosen line carried from Reu to Serug, the careful record of the generations approaching Abram, the line of faith traced through Serug toward the call of Abram) — Gill.

themes: And Reu lived two and thirty years, and begat Serug
Reu begetting Serug, continuing the line of promise
the line carried from Peleg through Reu to Serug
the genealogy narrowing toward the call of Abram

schools.summary: Reu's life: 'and Reu lived after he begat Serug two hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters' — Reu living two hundred and seven years after Serug's birth (a total of two hundred thirty-nine years, the shorter post-flood lifespan, like Peleg's, Reu begetting many other children besides Serug, the continuing and multiplying of the chosen line); Reu's total of two hundred thirty-nine years matching the shorter post-flood pattern of his father Peleg (v.19, the lifespans of the chosen line now settling around two hundred years, declining from the longer spans of Shem and Eber toward Abraham's day, the diminishing of human life by God's appointment); 'begat sons and daughters' (Reu having many children besides Serug, the line of promise traced through Serug, but the many other offspring noted, the multiplying of the chosen family, the increase of the line even as the lifespans shorten) — Gill.

themes: And Reu lived after he begat Serug two hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters
Reu's life and his many children
the shorter post-flood lifespan, settled around two hundred years
the line of promise carried through Serug

schools.summary: Serug begets Nahor: 'and Serug lived thirty years, and begat Nahor' — Serug (son of Reu) begetting Nahor at thirty years (the line of promise continuing from Reu through Serug to Nahor, the chosen line carried forward toward Abram, Nahor, the grandfather of Abram, and the namesake of Abram's brother, v.26); Serug begetting Nahor at thirty, and Serug's total lifespan being two hundred and thirty years (v.23, the shorter post-flood pattern, the line of promise carried through Nahor, the genealogy approaching Terah and Abram); 'begat Nahor' (Nahor, the son of Serug, the next in the line of promise, and the father of Terah, v.24, the chosen line carried from Serug to Nahor, the careful record of the generations immediately before Abram, the line of faith traced through Nahor toward the call of Abram) — Gill.

themes: And Serug lived thirty years, and begat Nahor
Serug begetting Nahor, continuing the line of promise
the line carried from Reu through Serug to Nahor
the genealogy nearing Terah and the call of Abram

schools.summary: Serug's life: 'and Serug lived after he begat Nahor two hundred years, and begat sons and daughters' — Serug living two hundred years after Nahor's birth (a total of two hundred and thirty years, the shorter post-flood lifespan, Serug begetting many other children besides Nahor, the continuing and multiplying of the chosen line); Serug's total of two hundred thirty years continuing the shorter post-flood pattern (like Peleg and Reu, the lifespans of the chosen line, declining from the longer spans of the fathers, now settling around two hundred years, the diminishing of human life as the genealogy approaches Abraham's day); 'begat sons and daughters' (Serug having many children besides Nahor, the line of promise traced through Nahor, but the many other offspring noted, the multiplying of the chosen family, the increase of the line) — Gill.

themes: And Serug lived after he begat Nahor two hundred years, and begat sons and daughters
Serug's life and his many children
the shorter post-flood lifespan, settled around two hundred years
the line of promise carried through Nahor

schools.summary: Nahor begets Terah: 'and Nahor lived nine and twenty years, and begat Terah' — Nahor (son of Serug) begetting Terah at twenty-nine years (the line of promise continuing from Serug through Nahor to Terah, the chosen line carried to Terah, the father of Abram, v.26, the genealogy reaching the immediate family of Abram); Nahor begetting Terah at twenty-nine, and Nahor's total lifespan being only one hundred forty-eight years (v.25, shorter still than the fathers before him, the post-flood lifespans continuing to decline, now toward one hundred fifty years, the diminishing of human life as the genealogy nears Abram); 'begat Terah' (Terah, the son of Nahor, the father of Abram, Nahor, and Haran, v.26, the chosen line carried to Terah, the genealogy reaching the household out of which God would call Abram, the line of faith traced through Terah to Abram) — Gill.

themes: And Nahor lived nine and twenty years, and begat Terah
Nahor begetting Terah, the father of Abram
the line carried from Serug through Nahor to Terah
the genealogy reaching the household out of which God would call Abram

schools.summary: Nahor's life: 'and Nahor lived after he begat Terah an hundred and nineteen years, and begat sons and daughters' — Nahor living one hundred and nineteen years after Terah's birth (a total of one hundred forty-eight years, the shortest lifespan yet in the post-flood line, Nahor begetting many other children besides Terah, the continuing and multiplying of the chosen line, with the lifespans now markedly diminished); Nahor's total of one hundred forty-eight years shorter than all the fathers before him in the post-flood line (the lifespans, declining from Shem's six hundred toward Abraham's one hundred seventy-five, 25:7, now reaching about one hundred fifty, the steady diminishing of human life by God's appointment as the genealogy nears Abram); 'begat sons and daughters' (Nahor having many children besides Terah, the line of promise traced through Terah, but the many other offspring noted, the multiplying of the chosen family) — Gill.

themes: And Nahor lived after he begat Terah an hundred and nineteen years, and begat sons and daughters
Nahor's life and his many children
the shortest lifespan yet, declining toward Abraham's day
the line of promise carried through Terah

schools.summary: Terah begets Abram, Nahor, and Haran: 'and Terah lived seventy years, and begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran' — Terah (son of Nahor) begetting three sons: Abram, Nahor, and Haran ('seventy years' marking the age at which Terah began to beget sons, Abram, though named first for his pre-eminence, likely not the eldest, being born when Terah was older, cf. 11:32; 12:4, the line of promise reaching Terah's son Abram); Terah's three sons — Abram (the head of the line of promise), Nahor (the namesake of his grandfather, and the grandfather of Rebekah and Laban), and Haran (the father of Lot, v.27) — named (Abram named first for his pre-eminence, not necessarily as the eldest, the household of Terah, out of which God would call Abram); 'Abram' (the chosen one, through whom the line of promise would continue and the great covenant be made, ch. 12; 15; 17, the genealogy of Shem, narrowing through the generations, reaching its goal in Abram, the man God would call out of Ur, v.31; 12:1, to be the father of the faithful and the line of the Messiah) — Gill, Clarke, JFB.

themes: And Terah lived seventy years, and begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran
Terah begetting Abram, Nahor, and Haran
Abram named first for his pre-eminence, the chosen one of the line of promise
the genealogy of Shem reaching its goal in Abram

schools.summary: The generations of Terah: 'now these are the generations of Terah: Terah begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran; and Haran begat Lot' — the formula introducing the history of Terah's family (and especially of Abram, the genealogy now becoming the history of the chosen family, from here the narrative following Abram, the father of the faithful, ch. 12 onward, the turning-point from the universal history to the line of promise); Terah's three sons again named — Abram, Nahor, and Haran (the household of Terah, the family from which God would call Abram, the chosen family at the head of the new section); 'Haran begat Lot' (Lot, the son of Haran, Abram's brother, and so Abram's nephew, Lot, who would accompany Abram from Ur and Haran, v.31; 12:4-5, and figure in the later history, chs. 13-14; 19, the introduction of Lot, Abram's nephew, who would share in Abram's journeyings) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Now these are the generations of Terah: Terah begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran; and Haran begat Lot
the generations of Terah and the family of Abram
the turn from the universal history to the line of promise
Lot, Abram's nephew, introduced

schools.summary: The death of Haran in Ur: 'and Haran died before his father Terah in the land of his nativity, in Ur of the Chaldees' — Haran (Abram's brother, the father of Lot) dying before his father ('before' meaning in his father's lifetime, Terah outliving his son, an early death, the first recorded instance of a son dying before his father, the death of Haran, leaving Lot fatherless, whom Abram would take with him); Haran dying 'in the land of his nativity' (in the land where he, and the family, were born, the family's native land Ur of the Chaldees, Haran dying in the homeland, before the family's migration toward Canaan, v.31); 'in Ur of the Chaldees' (Ur, a city of the Chaldees, in lower Mesopotamia/Babylonia, the original home of Terah's family and of Abram, the place from which God called Abram, Acts 7:2-4, Ur, a centre of the Chaldean civilization, and, by some accounts, of idolatry, Joshua 24:2, the native city of Abram, from which the family would set out for Canaan) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: And Haran died before his father Terah in the land of his nativity, in Ur of the Chaldees
the death of Haran in Ur of the Chaldees
Haran's early death, leaving Lot fatherless
Ur, the native city of Abram, from which God called him (Acts 7:2-4)

schools.summary: The marriages of Abram and Nahor: 'and Abram and Nahor took them wives: the name of Abram's wife was Sarai; and the name of Nahor's wife, Milcah, the daughter of Haran, the father of Milcah, and the father of Iscah' — the two surviving brothers (Haran having died, v.28) marrying (the families of Abram and Nahor, the marriages within the family of Terah, the setting of the household before the migration); 'Abram's wife was Sarai' (Sarai, later Sarah, 17:15, the wife of Abram, Sarai Abram's half-sister, the daughter of Terah by another mother, 20:12, though some identify her with Iscah, the wife of the father of the faithful, who would be the mother of the promised seed, though now barren, v.30); 'Nahor's wife, Milcah, the daughter of Haran... and the father of Iscah' (Nahor marrying Milcah, the daughter of Haran, his deceased brother, a marriage within the family, Haran also 'the father of Iscah,' some identifying Iscah with Sarai, though uncertain, the close family relations of Terah's house, Milcah the grandmother of Rebekah, 22:23, Isaac's future wife) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke.

themes: And Abram and Nahor took them wives: the name of Abram's wife was Sarai; and the name of Nahor's wife, Milcah
the marriages of Abram and Nahor
Sarai (later Sarah), Abram's half-sister and wife (20:12)
the close family ties of Terah's house, Milcah the grandmother of Rebekah (22:23)

schools.summary: The barrenness of Sarai: 'but Sarai was barren; she had no child' — Sarai, Abram's wife, barren and childless (the note set with emphasis, 'but,' marking the obstacle to the promise, the wife of the father of the faithful, through whom the promised seed must come, unable to bear children, the affliction that would make the promise of a seed, 12:2; 15:4; 17:16, a trial of faith); Sarai's barrenness a heavy affliction (childlessness being a grief and reproach, and it setting the stage for the great trial of Abram's faith — that God would promise a seed through a barren wife, the human impossibility against which God's promise and power would be displayed, Romans 4:19-21, Abraham believing in hope against hope); 'she had no child' (the childlessness of Sarai noted at the head of Abram's history, the promise of a numerous seed, 12:2, given to a man whose wife was barren, the long delay and the miraculous birth of Isaac, ch. 21, magnifying God's power and faithfulness, the barrenness of Sarai the backdrop against which the promise shines) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: But Sarai was barren; she had no child
the barrenness of Sarai
the obstacle to the promise, the human impossibility against God's power
the trial of faith, the backdrop against which the promise shines (Romans 4:19-21)

schools.summary: The departure from Ur: 'and Terah took Abram his son, and Lot the son of Haran his son's son, and Sarai his daughter in law, his son Abram's wife; and they went forth with them from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there' — the call of Abram first given in Ur (though Terah is named as the leader of the migration, taking Abram, Lot, and Sarai, the deeper cause God's call to Abram, given in Ur of the Chaldees before they came to Haran, Acts 7:2-4, 'The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Charran... Get thee out of thy country,' Abram, called by God, leading the family out of Ur toward Canaan, and Terah, as the aged head of the household, named as taking them, the departure from Ur in obedience to the divine call); a second reading taking the call of 12:1 as given at Haran, the move from Ur being Terah's own migration, but the dominant view, with Acts 7, placing the first call in Ur, with a renewed call at Haran (12:1); the family, setting out for Canaan, coming 'unto Haran (Charran), and dwelling there' (stopping at Haran, a city of Mesopotamia on the way toward Canaan, and settling for a time, whether because of Terah's age or other reasons, the migration pausing at Haran until Terah's death, v.32, after which Abram went on to Canaan, 12:4-5) — Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin.

debates.question: Was the call to Abram first given in Ur, or at Haran?

sides.position: First given in Ur of the Chaldees (Acts 7:2-4) — the departure from Ur already in obedience to it, with a renewed call at Haran (12:1) (the dominant view)
The move from Ur was Terah's own migration; the call to Abram given at Haran (12:1)

themes: And Terah took Abram his son, and Lot... and Sarai... and they went forth... from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there
the departure from Ur toward Canaan (the crux of the call of Abram)
the call first given in Ur (Acts 7:2-4) vs the call given at Haran
the migration halting at Haran until Terah's death

schools.summary: The death of Terah in Haran: 'and the days of Terah were two hundred and five years: and Terah died in Haran' — Terah's full life, two hundred and five years (longer than the immediately preceding generations, Nahor's 148, v.25, but in the shorter post-flood pattern, the death of Terah marking the end of the genealogy of Shem, vv.10-32, and the close of the family's stay at Haran); 'Terah died in Haran' (Terah dying at Haran, where the family had settled, v.31, he not reaching Canaan, but dying on the way, at the halting-place, after Terah's death Abram going on from Haran into Canaan, 12:4-5, the death of the aged father at Haran preceding Abram's onward pilgrimage); the death of Terah at Haran closing the genealogy and the prelude (with Terah dead, Abram, now the head of the family and called by God, 12:1, going forth from Haran into Canaan, to receive the promises, the death of Terah the threshold between the universal history, chs. 1-11, and the history of the patriarchs, chs. 12 onward, the close of the line of Shem and the dawn of the call of Abram) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the days of Terah were two hundred and five years: and Terah died in Haran
the death of Terah in Haran
the death of the aged father at the halting-place, before Canaan
the threshold between the universal history and the call of Abram (ch. 12)
